speaker: interviewer
text: This is Wednesday, March 6, 1996. I'm Michael [? Kelker, ?] interviewing Renate Vambery in St. Louis, Missouri. Can you tell us, please, how and why you describe yourself as an ethical humanist?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I don't follow any particular ritual, as you know. I was not brought up that way, for one thing. And I have not felt that comfortable in-- I've gone to churches and to synagogues and temples in the past, many long years ago, even in England, already. When I was in the service, I did go too, a few times. But I feel more comfortable with people who feel that we are the ones who have to correct the human condition or improve the human condition, that we have to do it, that I don't depend on a higher power to do that for me. That whatever needs to be done, or whatever I feel I have to do to help people or to help myself, that, that it has to come from me and from my colleagues and friends in the ethical society. And we do respect-- I like the fact that we respect all religions, and all origins, ethnic origins, and so on. But we still, we have this belief that it's up to the people on this earth now to change conditions and to, to make a difference.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you believe in a higher power?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm an agnostic. I don't know at this point. But I do believe that if I don't work for it for the betterment of the human condition, that if I don't work as hard as I can on in my friends at the Ethical Society or the Humanist Association-- incidentally, I chaired the Humanists for 11 years here, in St. Louis-- I feel it isn't going to happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you know, I feel, where was the higher power during the Holocaust? You know, you can ask that. Of course, I've been answered that by rabbis, that you have to have faith. And I don't think I have that kind of faith, that I can just go on that and say, well, it's all going to be taken care of. Because obviously it isn't. I see. Is there any general summary message that you have about your participation in a project like this? And I know this has just been one of, of several projects.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or anything to, to viewers who are hearing your testimony?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I have, you know, as I said, participated in the Holocaust. I was asked to serve on the commission, the Holocaust Commission, on the board, which I did. And then I started speaking in schools, because I felt that children needed to know. Although I think, sometimes, we try to get them too young. I don't think at 11 or 12 they're quite ready to take it. But that especially high school students need to know what can happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I'm also-- was very concerned about voting and trying to encourage high school seniors who are 18 to vote, since they have the vote now. I remember going to a school on the north side and asking the seniors, how many of you are 18 or over? And there was 3/4 of the class. And there had been an election the day before. This was a Wednesday and a Tuesday. And I said, how many of you voted yesterday? Nobody did. And nobody had voted. And I said, you see? Then I show them what can happen, like it did to my mother, that she was told she could no longer vote. And that it would be very disturbing if somebody here got a notice like that, and you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, so I felt I could contribute something to tell people how it started, and how the whole thing, the Holocaust, actually started in Germany, what happened, and to be sure, make sure that this doesn't happen anywhere else. Because it is happening in Bosnia. And we are not doing much. I belonged-- I was on the board of the United Nations Association. I served on the board, served many years on the World Federalist. And I'm somewhat discouraged. I feel that we haven't really gotten very far. We haven't really made much of a difference, in spite of, you know, since 1945. That's 50 years. And all these organizations that I belonged to-- I belonged to the Women's International League for Peace and Freedom at one time-- and I didn't like their stance on Israel. Because I have been in Israel twice. And I feel that it's a matter of survival every day, actually. Because I happened to be in Tel Aviv one day when there was a bomb scare. I was going to town just to bring back some presents, get a few presents, and couldn't take the same way back because there was a bomb scare.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I feel, you know, that they have-- I feel strongly, even though I'm not religiously Jewish, that Israel has to survive. And the people there have to survive. I was staying the second time, not in a hotel, but with friends from Germany. The man had-- he had been one of my friends from that German/Jewish youth group, and had been in for some time in Dachau concentration camp before he came to Israel. So I had a different viewpoint than some of the people in the, in the organization. And I said, I didn't want to hear their accusations all the time that it was all Israel's fault, the terrorism there. So I dissociated myself. I dissociated myself from the ACLU, the American Civil Liberties Union, because I could not agree, not only the fact that they wanted to defend the Nazis marching in Skokie and here. It was here in St. Louis.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I felt, OK, they have a right to march. And I said, they have a right to their day in court, but I'm not going to pay for it. So I said, let them pay for it if they want to go to court. They have a right to that. I know that. But I've disagreed on a number of things with the ACLU. I think they have gone too far. They are opposing now the uniforms in the schools. And I think it would help because we know that the, the people with certain colors are attacked, certain types of shoes or so, are murdered for it. And so I think it would be a good thing. I mean, they can still wear other clothes, you know, in their time off. But anyway, I understand the principle of the ACLU, the First Amendment. And I think it's important. But I think you can stretch it too far. And that's what I felt. And then I decide, you know, I'm not going to give money anymore. I'm not going to go. But there are enough-- you know, I've been in enough organizations, you know, to not always agree with everything. You can't do that. But I've-- it's been satisfying to serve on, on numerous boards. And professionally, also, I was part of a, part of a personal growth center that we had operated here for several years in the early '70s. I was the vice president. And this was in addition to my other regular job. And we did groups. And we showed humanistic psychology films. I have a whole literature on humanistic psychology. And we showed the film-- humanistic psychology films by well-known people. And I had taken a course at the University of Chicago, a three semester-- three trimester course in, in therapy. And I can't think of the name of it now, [INAUDIBLE] course. So I've been involved all my life. And I guess I want to be-- continue to be involved. Except I don't have the energy anymore, unfortunately, to go all day and all evening too, which I used to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, good. Thank you very much for participating in this project and speaking with us today.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome. Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's all yours. Would you explain this document please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This is a document came from the Lord Mayor of Stuttgart, where my parents lived. And was sent to my mother, Mathilde Liebman. I'm sure my father had the same. It's dated 13th of March, 1936. And it states that she cannot, that my mother can no longer vote because she is not of Aryan descent. The--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Can can flip it over.

speaker: interviewee
text: I can do that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And anytime.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, the exact translation is, 'We inform you herewith that you have been crossed off the list of voters, since our documents show that you are not of Aryan origin and, therefore, not entitled to vote.'

speaker: interviewer
text: And I'm going to stop. Anytime, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right. This is my American grandmother, my mother's mother, who was born in Pittsburgh, USA. She was 60 years old at that time, in 1920. Her name was Josie Neuburger, N-E-U-B-U-R-G-E-R, nee Frowenfeld, F-R-O-W-E-N-F-E-L-D. And she was the one who taught me the English nursery rhymes before I went to school.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And anytime.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right. She had gone with her family, from-- moved from Pittsburgh to Frankfurt, Germany because my great-grandfather thought that the climate was better, especially for his asthma. And then she, when she was old enough, she met and married my grandfather, who was a German, German/Jewish. And lived in Stuttgart, although she never liked it in Germany, as long as I knew her.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right. This picture was taken when my mother, who is in the middle, whose picture is in the middle, was 19. Her sister, on the left, Elsa, was 17. And the other sister, Irma, was 13. And so this was taken approximately 1903. And my mother, as you know, her name was Mathilde, or Tillie Liebman, L-I-E-B-M-A-N, nee Neuburger, N-E-U-B-U-R-G-E-R. My aunt Elsa, to the right of my mother, was-- had married Dr. Eugene Kauffman, K-A-U-F-F-M-A-N. And, and she's the one who was murdered in Auschwitz, as was her husband. The one on the, on my mother's left is Aunt Irma, who was married to the non-Jewish man, the Christian man who, who saved her in the basement of their home. Her name was Irma Maye, M-A-Y-E, also nee Neuburger. And she's the one who survived by being, as I mentioned, hidden. That this was all taken in Stuttgart, Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anytime. It's all yours.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right. This is my father, Dr. Walter Liebman, when he volunteered for World War I in 1914. His name was Walter, last name, Liebman. And this was, I don't know. This was taken in Germany somewhere. He was inducted into the army. And because he was Jewish, he was not immediately given officer status, which anyone with his educational level ordinarily would have been given. So in protest, he grew a beard, which I guess was not allowed in the army at the time. And he was then, strangely enough, promoted. And he's pictured on the other picture, in front, where he was then an officer in the German army and remained there for the next four years.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We've got that. And please explain this photograph.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. This is, again, my father as an officer in World War I. It's around 1915, taken in Stuttgart, with my, my mother on his side. Her name-- his name, Walter Liebman. Her name is Mathilde, or Tillie, Liebman. And my sister, Ruth, who was then three years old, or close to three years old, her name was Ruth Marianna Liebman. And later she, she was married and is now Ruth Szilaguri.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anytime.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. These are pictures taken during World War I of, first, on the, on this side, this is the right side, of my mother, Mathilde Liebman, with my sister, Ruth, and myself, Renate Liebman, at the time. It is probably 1916, taken 1916 or 1917. And on the other side is my father, as a lieutenant, with, also, my sister, Ruth Marianna Liebman, and my father, Walter Liebman, and myself, Renate Liebman around 1917.

speaker: interviewer
text: You can explain this photograph to me please?

speaker: interviewee
text: All right. This is a picture of my parents, Dr. Walter Liebman, and his-- and my mother, his wife, Mathilde Liebman, on vacation, while we were vacationing in Sulz, Germany-- S-U-L-Z. And I'm on the left of the picture, Renate, Renate Liebman at the time, and my sister, Ruth Liebman. We were both-- I was almost 8, and she was almost 12.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Would you explain this picture, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: All right. This is a picture taken in 1926 of Ruth and Renate Liebman. One-- Ruth is, at that time, 14 years old. And Renate is 10. And this was taken in Stuttgart, 1926. I said that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And explain this picture to me, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my grandparents at their golden wedding anniversary in 1933, beginning of '33, during happier times. My grandparents, in the middle, Josie and Fritz Neuburger, N-E-U-B-U-R-G-E-R. And in the back is a picture of myself, Renate Liebman, and my cousin, Edith Maye, M-A-Y-E. In front, in front of the grandparents is Edith's brother, Gunter, G-U-N-T-E-R, Maye, M-A-Y-E. And that was taken in 1933, in Stuttgart.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. All right.
